questions.title: Safe Division with Zero‑Division Handling
questions.scenario_framing: You are building a simple calculator that should never crash when the user tries to divide by zero.
questions.problem_statement: Implement a function `safe_divide(a, b)` that returns the result of `a / b`. If a division by zero would occur, the function should return the string "Cannot divide by zero!" instead of raising an exception.
questions.starter_code:
def safe_divide(a, b):
    """Return a / b, or a friendly message if b is zero."""
    # TODO: add try/except to handle ZeroDivisionError
    pass

rubric.name: Correct Result for Valid Input
rubric.description: Function returns the correct division result when b != 0.

rubric.name: Zero‑Division Handling
rubric.description: Function catches ZeroDivisionError and returns the exact message "Cannot divide by zero!".

rubric.name: Code Clarity
rubric.description: Code is readable, uses appropriate variable names, and includes a docstring.

rubric.name: No Unnecessary Exceptions
rubric.description: Function does not raise any exceptions for the tested inputs.

hints: Think about code that might fail and how to catch that specific failure.
Wrap the division statement in a try block and catch ZeroDivisionError.
Place `return a / b` inside the try block and `return "Cannot divide by zero!"` inside the except block.
questions.analogy_explanation: Imagine a vending machine that stops working when you try to buy an out‑of‑stock item. Instead of the machine breaking, it shows a friendly notice and stays ready for the next purchase. The try/except works the same way: it catches the problem and shows a helpful message.

questions.title: Fahrenheit‑to‑Celsius Converter with Input Validation
questions.scenario_framing: You are creating a thermostat app that lets users type a temperature in Fahrenheit and see the Celsius value. The app must stay responsive even if the user mistypes the number.
questions.problem_statement: Write a function `f_to_c(temp_str)` that receives a string, attempts to convert it to a float, then returns the Celsius temperature rounded to two decimals. If the string cannot be parsed as a number, return the string "Invalid temperature input!".
questions.starter_code:
def f_to_c(temp_str):
    """Convert Fahrenheit string to Celsius float, handling bad input."""
    # TODO: parse the string, catch ValueError, perform conversion
    pass

rubric.name: Correct Conversion
rubric.description: Function returns the correct Celsius value for valid numeric strings.

rubric.name: Input Validation
rubric.description: Function catches invalid numeric input and returns the exact error message.

rubric.name: Rounding Accuracy
rubric.description: Result is rounded to two decimal places.

rubric.name: Documentation & Style
rubric.description: Function includes a clear docstring and follows PEP‑8 naming.

hints: Parsing a string to a number can fail and raise an exception.
Use `float(temp_str)` inside a try block and catch `ValueError`.
Inside the try block compute `(float(temp_str) - 32) * 5/9`, round the result, and return it; in the except block return the error message.
questions.analogy_explanation: Think of the app as a kitchen scale: when you place a proper ingredient, it shows the weight; when you place a non‑weighable object, it beeps and tells you it can't measure it. The try/except lets the program “beep” and stay usable.

questions.title: Robust File Loader
questions.scenario_framing: Your program needs to load a configuration file supplied by the user. If the file is missing, the program should inform the user without crashing.
questions.problem_statement: Implement `load_file(path)` that attempts to open the file at `path` and returns its content as a string. If the file does not exist, catch the appropriate exception and return the string "File not found: <path>" (replace `<path>` with the actual argument).
questions.starter_code:
def load_file(path):
    """Read the file at *path* and return its contents, handling missing files gracefully."""
    # TODO: open the file, catch FileNotFoundError
    pass

rubric.name: Successful Read
rubric.description: Function returns the exact file content when the file exists.

rubric.name: Missing File Handling
rubric.description: Function catches the missing‑file error and returns the correctly formatted message.

rubric.name: Resource Management
rubric.description: File is opened using a context manager (`with` statement).

rubric.name: Docstring & Naming
rubric.description: Function includes a clear docstring and uses descriptive variable names.

hints: Opening a file can raise an exception if the file is not present.
Wrap the `open` call in a try block and catch `FileNotFoundError`.
Use `with open(path, 'r') as f: return f.read()` in the try block; in the except block return the formatted message.
questions.analogy_explanation: Imagine trying to open a locked cabinet: if the key is missing, you don't force it open; you politely note that the cabinet is unavailable. The try/except pattern does the same for files.

questions.title: Fix the Summation Logic
questions.scenario_framing: You wrote a function to sum numbers in a list, but it returns the wrong total. Your task is to locate and correct the logical mistake.
questions.problem_statement: Given the buggy function below, modify it so that `sum_list(nums)` returns the correct sum of all integers in the list `nums`. Do not change the function name or its signature.
questions.starter_code:
def sum_list(nums):
    """Return the sum of all numbers in *nums* (buggy implementation)."""
    total = 0
    for i in range(len(nums) - 1):
        total += nums[i]
    return total

# Example: sum_list([1, 2, 3, 4]) should return 10

rubric.name: Correct Output
rubric.description: Function returns the exact sum for any list of numbers, including empty lists.

rubric.name: Logical Fix
rubric.description: Bug is fixed by adjusting the loop or logic without adding unnecessary code.

rubric.name: Edge Cases
rubric.description: Function handles single‑element and empty lists correctly.

rubric.name: Code Clarity
rubric.description: Solution is clean, maintains docstring, and uses clear variable names.

hints: The loop currently stops before the last element.
Check the range used in the for‑loop; consider whether it should include the final index.
Change `range(len(nums) - 1)` to iterate over the entire list, e.g., `for num in nums:` or adjust the range to `len(nums)`.
questions.analogy_explanation: Think of counting books on a shelf: if you stop one short, you miss the last book. The bug is that the loop stops early, so the final element is never added.

questions.title: Batch Temperature Processor from File
questions.scenario_framing: You are building a data‑analysis tool that reads a file of Fahrenheit temperatures (one per line), converts each to Celsius, and returns a list of results. The tool must cope with a missing file and with lines that aren’t valid numbers.
questions.problem_statement:
Write a function `process_temperatures(path)` that:
1. Attempts to open the file at `path`. If the file is missing, return the string "File not found: <path>".
2. Reads each line, strips whitespace, and tries to convert it to a float.
3. For each valid number, converts it to Celsius using the formula `(F - 32) * 5/9` and rounds to two decimals.
4. Ignores lines that cannot be parsed as numbers (do not include them in the result list).
5. Returns the list of Celsius temperatures.

Example file content:
```
212
abc
32
```
`process_temperatures('temps.txt')` should return `[100.0, 0.0]`.
questions.starter_code:
def process_temperatures(path):
    """Read Fahrenheit values from *path*, convert to Celsius, handling errors gracefully.
    Returns a list of Celsius temperatures or an error message if the file is missing.
    """
    # TODO: implement the full workflow with appropriate try/except blocks
    pass

rubric.name: File‑Not‑Found Handling
rubric.description: Function returns the correctly formatted message when the file does not exist.

rubric.name: Line‑by‑Line Parsing
rubric.description: Function reads the file line by line and attempts conversion for each.

rubric.name: Invalid Line Skipping
rubric.description: Lines that cannot be parsed are ignored without causing a crash.

rubric.name: Accurate Conversion & Rounding
rubric.description: Each valid temperature is correctly converted to Celsius and rounded to two decimals.

rubric.name: Code Structure & Documentation
rubric.description: Solution uses clear variable names, context managers, and includes a helpful docstring.

hints: You need two separate try/except sections: one for opening the file and another for converting each line.
Inside the file‑reading block, iterate over each line, strip it, and try `float(line)`; catch `ValueError` to skip bad lines.
Perform the conversion `(f - 32) * 5/9`, round with `round(..., 2)`, and append to a result list; return that list at the end.
questions.analogy_explanation: Imagine a quality‑control line in a factory: the conveyor belt (file) may be missing, in which case the supervisor notes the issue. Each item (line) is inspected; defective items (non‑numeric lines) are set aside, while good items are processed (converted) and placed in a clean bin (result list).

    """Return a / b, or a friendly message if b is zero."""

result.feedback: The function currently contains only a placeholder and does not compute or return any result. Implement the division and add a try/except block to handle division by zero as specified.

rubric_scores.comment: The function does not perform any division and always returns None.
There is no try/except block, so the required message is never returned.
The code is readable, uses clear variable names, and includes a helpful docstring.
The function does not raise any exceptions for the tested inputs.

result.hint_to_show: Wrap the division in a try/except block catching ZeroDivisionError and return "Cannot divide by zero!" when it occurs.

result.feedback: The implementation incorrectly iterates only up to len(nums)-1, causing the last element (and single‑element lists) to be omitted. Adjust the loop to cover the full list. Otherwise the code style is fine.

No fix was applied; the loop still stops one element early.
It works for an empty list but fails for single‑element lists.
The code is readable, with a clear docstring and appropriate variable names.
